subtitle: Educational Bitcoin Simulator
serverWakeWarning: The server may take 30-60 seconds to start up if it was idle. If it seems slow, please be patient!
confirmResetPhase: Are you sure you want to reset this phase? All data will be deleted.
whoToSend: Who do you want to send to?
coinFileDescription: This is your digital coin file. You can modify it as you wish. Is this a problem or a feature?
transactionSentNegative: You sent {{amount}} coins to {{name}}. Your balance is negative... how can you send coins you don't have? 🤔
impossibleTransaction: This transaction seems impossible! {{name}} spent more coins than they had.
phaseEndMessage: We've seen that digital money can be easily copied. We need someone to verify transactions. But... who?
helpPhase0: In this phase, you'll explore why digital money can't be created as a simple file. You can edit your balance and make transactions without any verification. Discover what the problem is!
helpPhase1: Now we have a Bank that verifies transactions. You can no longer modify your balance directly. But... can we always trust the Bank?
helpIntro: BitQuest is an educational simulator that helps you understand Bitcoin and cryptocurrencies through hands-on experimentation.
helpHowToStartDesc: The teacher creates a room and shares the code with students. Everyone starts with 10 digital coins.
helpPhasesDesc: The game progresses through phases that introduce new concepts: from the double spending problem to proof of work.
waitingApproval: Request sent! Waiting for the Bank to process the transfer...
yourBalanceControlled: Your balance is controlled by the Bank. You cannot modify it.
transactionApproved: The Bank has approved the transfer! Your balance has been updated.
transactionRejected: The Bank has rejected the transfer.
bankUnavailable: The Bank is unavailable. Transfers cannot be processed.
phase1Question: The Bank is fast and efficient... but can we always trust it? What if it doesn't let us send money?
discrepancyDetected: Discrepancy detected! A student has manually modified their coin file.
maxAmountExceeded: For your security, you must visit the office in person to send amounts greater than {{maxAmount}} coins.
phase0InstructionTitle: Instructions for Phase 0: The double spending problem
phase1InstructionTitle: Instructions for Phase 1: The centralized Bank
phase0Instruction1: In this phase, students discover the double spending problem.
phase0Instruction2: They can freely edit their coin files and make transactions without any verification.
phase0Activity1: Ask a student to send coins honestly
phase0Activity2: Ask what happens if they don't subtract from their balance
phase0Activity4: Discuss why this system doesn't work
phase1Instruction1: In this phase, we introduce a centralized Bank that controls all balances.
phase1Instruction2: Transfers are queued and may take time to process (like in the real world).
phase1Activity1: Approve some valid transfers to demonstrate functionality
phase1Activity2: Reject a transfer arbitrarily to demonstrate censorship
phase1Activity3: Disconnect the Bank temporarily to show single point of failure
phase1Activity4: Discuss: can we create a system without depending on a Bank?
phaseInstructionsComingSoon: Instructions for this phase coming soon...
sharedLedgerInfo: This ledger is the same for all participants
proposalSent: Proposal sent to the network. Waiting for votes from other participants...
transactionAcceptedConsensus: Transaction accepted by consensus! The ledger has been updated for everyone.
falseProposalWarning: You proposed a transaction on behalf of another person. How can others be sure this proposal is legitimate?
consensusQuestion: Distributed consensus works... but how can we authenticate who sends each transaction?
phase2Question: If we don't want a central Bank, how can we agree on which transactions are valid?
phase2QuestionFinal: Now that everyone can see the ledger, how can we be sure that a message really comes from who claims to be its author?
phase2Instruction1: In this phase, we remove the central Bank and introduce a shared ledger.
phase2Instruction2: All students can propose transactions and vote to accept or reject them.
phase2Activity1: Let them propose honest transactions and see how consensus works
phase2Activity2: Ask some to vote maliciously (observe that the honest majority wins)
phase2Activity3: Have someone propose a "fake" transaction on behalf of another
phase2Activity4: Discuss: How can we authenticate who sends each transaction?
phase3.noKeysYet: You haven't generated your keys yet
phase3.keysReady: Keys generated and broadcast to the network
phase3.keysGenerated: Keys generated! Now broadcast your public key to the network.
phase3.keyNotBroadcastYet: Key generated locally — not broadcast yet
phase3.broadcastKey: Broadcast public key to the network
phase3.keyBroadcast: Public key broadcast! Everyone can now see it.
phase3.messagePlaceholder: Ex: I give 5 coins to Bob
phase3.fraudWarning: This message does NOT come from who claims to be its author. Possible fraud!
phase3.generateKeysFirst: You must first generate your keys to sign messages.
phase3.signFirst: You must sign the message before sending it.
phase3.signatureCreated: Signature created! Only you can create this signature because only you have the private key.
phase3.hashTooltip: Converts the message into a unique fixed-size digital fingerprint
phase3.signatureTooltip: Encrypts the hash with the private key to prove authorship
phase3.verifyTooltip: Decrypts the signature with the public key to check authenticity
phase3.selectMessageToInspect: Select a message from the channel to see its full anatomy
phase3.onlyYouCanSign: Only you can sign with d
phase3.tooltipN: We multiply the two primes to get n. This number is part of the public key and makes it very hard to discover the original p and q (factoring).
phase3.tooltipPhi: Euler's φ function counts how many numbers less than n share no factors with n. We need φ to find the keys, but it is NOT part of the public key.
phase3.tooltipE: The public exponent must be coprime with φ(n), meaning they share no common factor (their GCD must be 1). It is chosen randomly among valid candidates.
phase3.tooltipD: The private exponent is the modular inverse of e: the number that multiplied by e gives remainder 1 when divided by φ(n). It is the "secret" that allows signing.
phase3.demoMessagePlaceholder: Type a message to see how it gets signed...
phase3.demoHashExplain: The hash compresses any message into a short fixed-size number. Change a single letter and the hash changes completely.
phase3.demoSignExplain: Signing = raise the hash to the private key (d) modulo n. Only whoever has d can create this signature.
phase3.demoVerifyExplain: Verify = raise the signature to the public key (e) modulo n. If the result matches the hash, the signature is authentic.
phase3.demoVerifyMatch: Valid signature! The recovered hash matches.
phase3.demoVerifyFail: Invalid signature! The hash does not match.
phase3.hashInputPlaceholder: E.g.: Hello, this is a test!
phase3.hashExplainIntro: A hash is like a digital fingerprint: it compresses any text (short or long) into a fixed-size code. If you change a single letter, the fingerprint changes completely.
phase3.hashTryChanging: Try changing a single letter and watch how the entire hash changes!
phase3.hashInitExplain: Like SHA-256, our mini-hash starts with a fixed value (H0). From there, each letter of the message transforms it.
phase3.tooltipHashInit: Every hash function starts with a fixed initial state. SHA-256 uses 8 32-bit values; our mini-hash uses one 24-bit value. It's like the starting point of a mix.
phase3.tooltipHashAscii: Each letter is converted to its ASCII code (a number). Then it's multiplied by a constant (0x1F3D) to spread the bits.
phase3.tooltipHashXor: XOR (⊕) combines the current state with the processed letter. If two bits are the same it gives 0, if different it gives 1. This mixes the data.
phase3.tooltipHashRotate: Rotating the bits 7 positions to the left spreads the information across the entire number. It's like shuffling cards.
phase3.tooltipHashAddK: We add a different constant K in each round (there are 8 that cycle). This adds more variety to the mix.
fakeMessagePlaceholder: Ex: I'm Alice and I give 100 coins to Eve
phase3Instruction1: In this phase, we introduce public key cryptography and digital signatures.
phase3Instruction2: Each student generates a key pair (public/private) to authenticate their messages.
phase3Activity1: Send a fake message to demonstrate the initial problem
phase3Activity2: Let students generate their keys and sign messages
phase3Activity3: Verify signatures as a group and detect fake messages
phase3Activity4: Discuss: Why must the private key be secret?
name: Name
phase4.txSigned: Transaction signed! Review it and decide whether to broadcast.
phase4.txSignedHint: You can still cancel. Once broadcast, all nodes will process it.
phase4.noUtxos: You have no UTXOs. The teacher will send you coins.
phase4.utxosInitialized: UTXOs initialized! You have 3 UTXOs with values of 10, 5 and 2 BTC.
phase4.selectAtLeastOneUtxo: You must select at least one UTXO
phase4.addAtLeastOneOutput: You must add at least one output
phase4.outputsExceedInputs: Outputs ({{outputs}} BTC) exceed inputs ({{inputs}} BTC). Adjust the amounts.
phase4.utxoAlreadySpent: UTXO already spent: {{utxoId}}. You cannot spend it twice.
phase4.transactionSuccess: Valid transaction! Your UTXOs have been marked as spent and new UTXOs have been created.
phase4.selectedUtxos: You have selected UTXOs for a total of {{total}} BTC. Now define the outputs.
phase4.noVerifiedYet: You haven't verified any transactions yet
phase4.question: Now that we know how transactions work with UTXOs, how do we organize them so everyone can verify them and no one can modify the history?
phase4Panel.title: How UTXOs Work
phase4Panel.block1Intro: In Bitcoin, there are no "balances" like in a bank. Your wealth is the sum of individual bills called UTXOs (Unspent Transaction Outputs).
phase4Panel.tooltipBankModel: A bank keeps a balance number. If you have €17, the bank simply says "17". You don't know where they come from.
phase4Panel.tooltipUtxoModel: In Bitcoin, you have individual "bills". To know your balance, you add up all the bills you have.
phase4Panel.tooltipUtxoBill: Each UTXO is like a bill that can only be spent whole. You can't "split" a bill.
phase4Panel.block2Intro: To send BTC, you must take whole UTXOs as "inputs", create new UTXOs as "outputs", and return your change.
phase4Panel.tooltipSelectInputs: UTXOs are consumed whole. If you want to send 7 BTC but your bill is 10, you must use the entire bill.
phase4Panel.tooltipDefineOutputs: New UTXOs are created for the recipients. Each output is a new "bill".
phase4Panel.tooltipConservation: The sum of inputs must be ≥ the sum of outputs. You can't create BTC from nothing!
phase4Panel.tooltipChange: If the input is larger than the output, the surplus comes back to you as a new change UTXO.
phase4Panel.block3Intro: Each transaction is signed with your private key. This proves you own the UTXOs you're spending.
phase4Panel.noKeysWarning: You need to generate keys in Phase 3 first to see this demo.
phase4Panel.tooltipHash: First we hash the transaction to get a unique fixed-size summary.
phase4Panel.tooltipSign: We sign the hash with the private key: hash^d mod n. Only you can do this.
phase4Panel.tooltipVerify: Anyone can verify with your public key: signature^e mod n. If the result matches the hash, the signature is valid.
phase4Panel.tooltipResult: If the recovered hash matches the original hash, the signature is valid and the transaction is authentic.
phase4Panel.block4Intro: The UTXO model solves the double-spend problem: each UTXO can only be spent once. Once consumed, it's marked and cannot be reused.
phase4Panel.firstSpendDesc: The UTXO is available, the transaction is processed.
phase4Panel.doubleSpendDesc: The UTXO is already consumed, the transaction is rejected.
phase4Panel.demoTitle: Interactive demo: try to spend twice!
phase4Panel.tooltipDemoCheck: Before processing, we check if the UTXO is still available.
phase4Panel.tooltipDemoTx: The transaction is valid because the UTXO was not spent.
phase4Panel.tooltipDemoMark: Once processed, the UTXO is marked as spent (isSpent: true).
phase4Panel.tooltipDemoCheckAgain: We check the UTXO status again...
phase4Panel.tooltipDemoAlready: The UTXO was already consumed in the previous transaction.
phase4Panel.tooltipDemoRejected: The network rejects any attempt to spend an already consumed UTXO.
phase4Panel.demoRejectedReason: Cannot spend a UTXO that has already been consumed.
phase4Panel.whyNotFlip: Why can't we just set isSpent: false again?
phase4Panel.whyNotFlipExplain: Because once you broadcast the transaction, ALL nodes in the network mark that UTXO as spent. You can't "unmark" it on your computer and fool the rest — each one has their own copy and will reject your fake transaction.
phase4Panel.tooltipStepSign: First you sign the transaction with your private key. This happens on your device, offline if needed (like a hardware wallet).
phase4Panel.tooltipStepBroadcast: You send the signed TX to the network. All nodes receive and validate it.
phase4Panel.tooltipStepConsensus: Each node marks the UTXO as spent. Now EVERYONE knows it cannot be reused.
phase4Panel.tooltipDemoBroadcast: The transaction propagates to all nodes. Each one updates their record.
phase4Panel.demoBroadcastDesc: All nodes mark UTXO#A1 as spent
phase4Panel.demoAllNodesReject: All nodes reject: UTXO#A1 is already recorded as spent
phase4Instruction1: In this phase, we introduce the UTXO (Unspent Transaction Outputs) model from Bitcoin.
phase4Instruction2: Each coin is a unique piece that is consumed and created in each transaction.
phase4Activity1: Make an exact payment transaction (UTXO = amount)
phase4Activity2: Make a transaction with change (UTXO > amount)
phase4Activity3: Combine multiple UTXOs in one transaction
phase4Activity4: Try to spend a UTXO twice to see the error
phase5.needMiners: We need miners to confirm them!
phase5.transactionCreated: Transaction created! Propagating through the network...
phase5.transactionReceived: Transaction received by all nodes. Now waiting for confirmation.
phase5.nodeDisconnected: Your node is disconnected from the network
phase5.propagationInfo: Transactions propagate from node to node until reaching everyone
phase5.notConfirmedWarning: These transactions are NOT confirmed yet. They need to be included in a block.
phase5.noTransactionsYet: No transactions in the mempool yet
phase5.question: If there are hundreds of transactions waiting, who decides which ones get included first in a block?
phase5Instruction1: Each student represents a NODE in the Bitcoin network.
phase5Instruction2: Transactions propagate from node to node until reaching everyone.
phase5Activity2: Disconnect a node and see how transactions don't reach it
phase5Activity3: Fill the mempool and discuss: who decides which ones get confirmed first?
phase5Activity4: Introduce the concept of fees as priority
phase6.nonceHint: You can edit it manually or click to increment
phase6.hashStartsWith: Find a hash that starts with
phase6.submitInfo: You can submit the block at any time, but it will only be accepted if the hash is valid and you are first.
phase6.tooLateError: "Too late! Someone already mined this block."
phase6.submitQuickly: Submit it quickly before someone else finds another!
phase6.foundValidHash: You found a valid hash! Submit it to the network!
phase6.tooLate: Too late! Someone already mined this block.
phase6.edu1: The block contains transactions + the previous block hash
phase6.edu2: We add a value (nonce) and calculate the SHA-256 hash
phase6.edu3: If the hash starts with {{zeros}} zeros → valid block! (difficulty)
phase6.waitingForGenesis: Waiting for the session to start
phase6.teacherWillStart: The teacher will create the genesis block to start mining.
phase6.waitingForBlock: No block to mine. Press the button to create one!
phase6Panel.title: How does Blockchain work?
phase6Panel.section1Intro: In the previous phase we saw that each network node has its own mempool with pending transactions. But each node may have transactions in a different order, or even transactions that others don't have!
phase6Panel.section1Need: We need a way to agree on which order is the "good" one: a block that everyone accepts as valid.
phase6Panel.section1Diff1: The order of transactions is different at each node
phase6Panel.section1Diff3: {{nodeC}} has TX#6, which the others don't have
phase6Panel.section1Solution: Someone has to do the work of ordering and grouping transactions into a block, and prove they've done computational effort to do it. In return, they receive a reward. This is what we call mining!
phase6Panel.section2Intro: A hash is a digital fingerprint of any text. If you change a single letter, the hash changes completely. Try it!
phase6Panel.section2Note: Notice that the hash always has the same length (64 hexadecimal characters), regardless of whether the text is short or long. And any small change produces a completely different hash!
phase6Panel.section3Intro: To mine a block, we need to find a number (nonce) that, combined with the block data, produces a hash starting with {{target}}. This requires computational effort: trying many nonces until we find one that works!
phase6Panel.nonceExplain: The nonce (Number used ONCE) is a number that only serves to achieve a hash that meets the difficulty requirement. The more leading zeros we require, the harder it is to find a valid nonce and the more computation time is needed.
phase6Panel.section4Intro: Each block contains the hash of the previous block, creating a chain. If you modify any data in a block, its hash changes and all subsequent blocks become invalid. That's why it's called a blockchain!
phase6Panel.section4Warning: Try changing the data in Block #1. You'll see how the hash changes and the block becomes invalid (red). But also, blocks #2 and #3 become invalid too because they depend on the previous hash! You'll need to re-mine each block in order.
phase6Instruction1: Students compete to find a NONCE that produces a valid hash.
phase6Instruction2: The first one to find a valid hash wins the reward.
phase6Activity1: Mining race: everyone mines the same block, who wins?
phase6Activity2: Try to submit blocks with invalid hash to see rejection
phase6Activity3: Analyze statistics: is who wins fair?
phase6Activity4: Discuss: how do we prevent someone from flooding the network with fake blocks?
phase7Instruction1: Now your machines mine automatically! The system looks for hashes below a target value (e.g., 00F2). The smaller that value, the harder it is to find a valid hash.
phase7Instruction2: Every few blocks, the system checks if blocks came too fast or too slow and adjusts the difficulty. This is exactly what real Bitcoin does!
phase7Activity1: Turn machines on and off: what happens to the time between blocks when there are more or fewer miners on the network?
phase7Activity2: Watch the difficulty panel: when the average time is very different from the target, what do you think will happen at the next adjustment?
phase7Activity3: Why do you think Bitcoin needs this mechanism? What would happen if difficulty stayed the same forever?
phase7.adjustmentInfo: Difficulty adjusts every {{interval}} blocks. Target is one block every {{target}} seconds.
phase7Activity4: Look at the period history: does the system manage to self-regulate? How does it do it?
pool.title: Mining Pools
pool.name: Pool name
phase8Instruction1: Block reward halves every certain number of blocks (halving)
phase8Instruction2: Miners earn transaction fees in addition to block rewards
phase8Activity2: Select higher-fee transactions as a miner
phase8Activity3: Observe halvings and how they affect mining
phase8.feeInfo: Higher fees = higher priority for miners
phase9Instruction1: In Bitcoin we don't send money to names, but to addresses. Nobody knows who owns an address!
phase9Instruction2: Generate addresses, ask your classmates for theirs, and send BTC. Be careful: if you get the address wrong, you lose them forever.
phase9Activity1: 51% Attack: Try to reverse transactions with more than 50% of hash power
phase9Activity2: Extreme Congestion: Create many transactions and observe the fee market
phase9Activity3: Fork: Watch how competing chains are resolved
phase9Activity4: Sustainable Economy: Analyze if fees can maintain security
phase9Activity5: Environmental Impact: Calculate how much energy we spent
phase0StudentDesc: You have a file with 10 coins. You can send coins to classmates and edit your file freely. Experiment!
phase0StudentActivity1: Send coins to a classmate and update your file
phase0StudentActivity2: What happens if you don't subtract from your balance?
phase0StudentActivity3: Can you spend more than you have?
phase0StudentActivity4: Why does this system have problems?
phase1StudentDesc: Now a Bank controls all transactions. You must request transfers and wait for approval.
phase1StudentActivity1: Request a transfer to a classmate
phase1StudentActivity3: What happens if the Bank disconnects?
phase1StudentActivity4: What are the drawbacks of depending on a Bank?
phase2StudentDesc: Transactions are approved by a vote of all participants. Propose and vote on transactions.
phase2StudentActivity3: Observe how collective decisions are made
phase2StudentActivity4: What problems does this system have?
phase3StudentDesc: Generate your key pair and sign your messages to prove your identity.
phase3StudentActivity1: Generate your public and private keys
phase3StudentActivity4: Can you detect a fake message?
phase4StudentDesc: Learn how Bitcoin manages balances with the UTXO (Unspent Transaction Outputs) model.
phase4StudentActivity2: Create a transaction by selecting inputs and outputs
phase4StudentActivity3: Observe how change UTXOs are generated
phase4StudentActivity4: Why is the UTXO model useful?
phase5StudentDesc: You are a network node. Create transactions and watch how they propagate through the mempool.
phase5StudentActivity2: Watch the propagation across the network
phase5StudentActivity3: What happens if a node disconnects?
phase6StudentTitle: Phase 6: Mining and Proof of Work
phase6StudentDesc: Compete to find a valid NONCE and mine blocks. The first to find it wins the reward!
phase6StudentActivity2: Search for a NONCE that produces a valid hash
phase7StudentDesc: Your machines mine automatically! The system adjusts difficulty to keep a steady block rate, just like real Bitcoin.
phase7StudentActivity1: Turn machines on and off: what happens to block times?
phase7StudentActivity2: Watch the difficulty panel: what will happen at the next adjustment?
phase7StudentActivity3: Why does Bitcoin need this mechanism?
phase7StudentActivity4: Check the period history: does the system self-regulate?
phase8StudentDesc: Send transactions with fees and watch how the system prioritizes them. Higher fee transactions get included first!
phase8StudentActivity1: Create transactions with different fees and watch which ones get included first
phase8StudentActivity2: What happens when the mempool fills up? How can you prioritize your tx?
phase8StudentActivity3: Observe halvings: what happens when the block reward drops?
phase9StudentDesc: Send BTC using anonymous addresses instead of names. Discover how privacy works in Bitcoin.
phase9StudentActivity1: Generate a new address to receive BTC
phase9StudentActivity2: Ask a classmate for their address to send them BTC
phase9StudentActivity3: Look at the blockchain: do you know who owns each address?
phase9StudentActivity4: Be careful: if you get the address wrong, the BTC is lost forever!
phase9StudentActivity5: Reflect: is Bitcoin anonymous or pseudonymous?
phase9.title: Addresses & Pseudonymity
phase9.subtitle: Send BTC to addresses, not names
phase9.noAddresses: You have no addresses. The teacher must initialize the phase.
phase9.inputsLabel: INPUTS (what I spend) — Select UTXOs from the panel above
phase9.needOutput: Add at least one output with address and amount
phase9.insufficientInputs: Insufficient inputs: outputs + fee exceed total
